questions.question: Why did the British introduce the Permanent Settlement?
questions.answer: To ensure a fixed and regular income from land revenue.

questions.question: What responsibility did zamindars have under the Permanent Settlement?
questions.answer: They had to collect land revenue from peasants and pay it to the British.

questions.question: Why did many zamindars lose their lands during British rule?
questions.answer: They failed to pay the fixed revenue demanded by the British.

questions.question: What happened to zamindari lands when revenue was not paid?
questions.answer: The lands were auctioned by the British government.

questions.question: Why did peasants often fall into debt?
questions.answer: Because of heavy land taxes and borrowing from moneylenders.

questions.question: Why did European planters want peasants to grow indigo?
questions.answer: Indigo produced blue dye used in European textile industries.

questions.question: What problem did peasants face after taking advances from planters?
questions.answer: They were forced to grow indigo instead of food crops.

questions.question: Why did peasants prefer growing food crops instead of indigo?
questions.answer: Food crops were necessary for their survival.

questions.question: How did indigo cultivation affect the soil?
questions.answer: It reduced soil fertility.

questions.question: What was one major reason for the Indigo Revolt?
questions.answer: Forced cultivation of indigo by European planters.

questions.question: How did peasants protest during the Indigo Revolt?
questions.answer: They refused to grow indigo.

questions.question: Why were moneylenders important in rural society?
questions.answer: They provided loans to peasants.

questions.question: Why were peasants dependent on moneylenders?
questions.answer: They needed money to pay taxes and farming expenses.

questions.question: Which region became the centre of indigo cultivation?
questions.answer: Bengal.

questions.question: Why was indigo considered a cash crop?
questions.answer: It was grown mainly for trade and profit.

questions.question: What action did the British government take after the Indigo Revolt?
questions.answer: They set up the Indigo Commission.

questions.question: What was the purpose of the Indigo Commission?
questions.answer: To investigate the problems faced by peasants.

questions.question: How did colonial policies affect Indian agriculture?
questions.answer: They encouraged cash crop cultivation instead of food crops.

questions.question: Why were peasants unhappy with European planters?
questions.answer: Planters forced them to grow indigo and paid low prices.

questions.question: What was one effect of heavy land revenue demands?
questions.answer: Many peasants fell into poverty and debt.

questions.question: Who benefited most from indigo cultivation?
questions.answer: European planters.

questions.question: Why did indigo cultivation reduce food production?
questions.answer: Land used for indigo could not be used for food crops.

questions.question: What does the Indigo Revolt show about peasants?
questions.answer: They could unite and protest against exploitation.

questions.question: How did colonial land policies affect rural society?
questions.answer: They increased economic pressure on peasants.

questions.question: Why is the Indigo Revolt important in Indian history?
questions.answer: It showed organized resistance by peasants against colonial exploitation.
